title: Module Check: Binary Search, Heaps, and Monotonic Stacks

questions.question: In the unified binary search template `while lo < hi`, what value does `lo` hold when the loop exits, and why is this useful?
questions.answer: lo == hi, pointing to the single remaining candidate — the answer position. This is useful because you don't need a separate post-loop check for a found/not-found case; the loop terminates with lo as the target index regardless of whether you're doing exact search or finding a boundary.

questions.question: To find the K largest elements in a stream, do you use a min-heap or max-heap of size K? Explain the reasoning.
questions.answer: A min-heap of size K. The root is always the Kth largest seen so far. When a new element arrives, compare it to the root: if larger, pop the root and push the new element. This maintains the K largest with O(log K) per insertion. A max-heap of size K would require you to pop K times to get the answer.

questions.question: Why is heapify O(n) rather than O(n log n), even though each sift-down is O(log n)?
questions.answer: In bottom-up heapify, most nodes are near the leaves and have small subtrees. The sift-down cost per node is proportional to its subtree height, and half the nodes are leaves (height 0). The sum converges as a geometric series: n/2·0 + n/4·1 + n/8·2 + … = O(n).

questions.question: What is the recognition signal for a monotonic stack problem?

options.A: The problem asks for a subarray with maximum sum
options.B: For each element, find the nearest element to the left or right that is greater or smaller
options.C: The problem involves merging overlapping intervals
options.D: The problem requires finding the median of a stream

questions.explanation: Monotonic stacks solve 'next greater/smaller element' queries in O(n) by maintaining a stack where elements are always in monotone order. Max subarray sum uses Kadane's; intervals use sorting; stream median uses two heaps.

questions.question: Which midpoint formula prevents integer overflow in binary search?
questions.explanation: `(lo + hi)` overflows when both values are near INT_MAX. `lo + (hi - lo) // 2` computes the offset first, keeping all values within range. Option C is equivalent but less idiomatic. Option A and D both suffer the same overflow risk.
